coredata.dc:title: Fe, N-CQDs triggered the fabrication of alginate encapsulated g-C3N4 hydrogel for efficient photocatalytic activation of PMS and antibiotic degradation

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) mediated g-C3N4 (CN) is a promising visible-light-driven semiconductor in catalyzing peroxymonosulfate (PMS) for aqueous contaminants remediation. However, the poor dispersibility of powered catalyst and its challenging recyclability impede their broader application. Herein, we embedded FeN bridge within the g-C3N4 framework and immobilized g-C3N4 gel beads (CA/FNCCN) through a 3D cross-linking process with sodium alginate. Alginate can serve as a stabilizing carrier, preserving the crystalline structure of FNCCN through the formation of H bonds between alginate and FNCCN. The resulting beads displayed significantly improved photo-electrochemical performance, along with greatly enhanced adsorption capacity and catalytic activity toward Sulfamethoxazole (SMX). The catalytic efficiency of CA/FNCCN was markedly increased as compared to non-immobilized FNCCN due to reduced photo-carrier recombination and accelerated electron transport. CA/FNCCN-1 can degrade 99.18 % of SMX within 40 mins, with noteworthy reduced Fe leaching. The primary active species in the system were 1O2 and h+, and the direct electron transfer between PMS and SMX mediated by alginate also contributed to the removal of SMX. Additionally, the degradation pathway of SMX was elucidated through LC-MS and DFT calculations. Simultaneously, FNCCN integrated alginate hydrogel can remain active without decay after 10 cycles.

head.ce:title: Fe, N-CQDs triggered the fabrication of alginate encapsulated g-C3N4 hydrogel for efficient photocatalytic activation of PMS and antibiotic degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Architecture, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Architecture, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200093, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) mediated g-C3N4 (CN) is a promising visible-light-driven semiconductor in catalyzing peroxymonosulfate (PMS) for aqueous contaminants remediation. However, the poor dispersibility of powered catalyst and its challenging recyclability impede their broader application. Herein, we embedded FeN bridge within the g-C3N4 framework and immobilized g-C3N4 gel beads (CA/FNCCN) through a 3D cross-linking process with sodium alginate. Alginate can serve as a stabilizing carrier, preserving the crystalline structure of FNCCN through the formation of H bonds between alginate and FNCCN. The resulting beads displayed significantly improved photo-electrochemical performance, along with greatly enhanced adsorption capacity and catalytic activity toward Sulfamethoxazole (SMX). The catalytic efficiency of CA/FNCCN was markedly increased as compared to non-immobilized FNCCN due to reduced photo-carrier recombination and accelerated electron transport. CA/FNCCN-1 can degrade 99.18 % of SMX within 40 mins, with noteworthy reduced Fe leaching. The primary active species in the system were 1O2 and h+, and the direct electron transfer between PMS and SMX mediated by alginate also contributed to the removal of SMX. Additionally, the degradation pathway of SMX was elucidated through LC-MS and DFT calculations. Simultaneously, FNCCN integrated alginate hydrogel can remain active without decay after 10 cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfamethoxazole, a typical sulfonamide antibiotic, is widely used for treating infections caused by sensitive bacteria in the urinary, intestinal, and biliary tracts (Li et al., 2021). However, due to the misuse and discharge of pharmaceuticals, SMX has been detected in various water bodies worldwide. The long-term accumulation of residual SMX not only negatively impacts the aquatic ecosystem but also induces the formation of resistance genes, posing potential health risks to humans. Liu et al. (2014) found that SDZ, SMX, and SMR exhibited genotoxicity in goldfish (Carassius auratus); Chen et al. (2020) observed genotoxic damage in Chlorella vulgaris after exposure to various SAs (i.e., SDZ, SMR, and SMT) at a concentration of 270 mg/L. Therefore, developing efficient, safe, and reliable water treatment technologies to remove SMX is of great importance and interest.

Compared to traditional water treatment technologies, such as biodegradation, physical adsorption, and chemical precipitation, sulfate radical-based advanced oxidation processes (SR-AOPs) have been increasingly applied to the treatment of wastewater containing recalcitrant organic compounds in recent years (Li et al., 2021). SR-AOPs offer several advantages, such as short reaction cycles, effective degradation, minimal production of toxic byproducts, and high reaction rates, which make SR-AOPs as a superior choice for addressing complex wastewater treatment challenges (Tian et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2021). In practical applications, SR-AOPs typically require external energy inputs or electron transfer reactions to cleave peroxide bonds in persulfate molecules, thus generating highly oxidative SO4·− for efficient pollutant removal. Common methods for activating persulfates include ultrasonic, thermal, ultraviolet, carbon-based material, transition metal, and photocatalytic activation (Wang et al., 2021). Among them, visible-light-driven photocatalytic activation is particularly valued for its superior treatment efficacy, potential for solar energy utilization, and mild reaction conditions (Chen et al., 2021).

Graphitic carbon nitride ( g -C3N4), as a novel photocatalyst, features adjustable band gaps, straightforward synthesis, and high thermal and chemical stability, making it widely used in photocatalysis and persulfate activation (Yang et al., 2023). However, the practical application of g-C3N4 as an efficient photocatalyst is severely limited due to its narrow visible light response range, rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers, and a tendency to aggregate, display poor dispersibility and complicated recovery in water (Balakrishnan & Chinthala, 2022). The development of g-C3N4 based photocatalysts with high catalytic activity and easy recovery was especially crucial. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel type of carbon nanomaterial, are characterized by their excellent water solubility, superior biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, stable photoluminescence, and a wide and continuous excitation spectrum (Yue et al., 2023). As electron acceptors and spectral converters, CQDs can effectively modify g-C3N4 and enhance its photocatalytic activity. Additionally, the chemical bonds formed by covalently attaching heteroatom-doped CQDs to g-C3N4 serve as efficient electron transport channels for photo-generated carries separation (Hu et al., 2023; Mou et al., 2019; Wang, Chen, et al., 2023; Wang, Zhang, & Li, 2023). This configuration reduces the interfacial barrier between the hydrophobic g-C3N4 and the hydrophilic CQDs components, minimizing charge transfer losses and facilitating more efficient and rapid charge movement. However, similar to most modification techniques, the composite material created by modifying g-C3N4 with heteroatom-doped CQDs, while showing enhanced photocatalytic activity, still presents as a loose and lightweight powder, which results in severe catalyst aggregation, poor repeatability, and a cumbersome recovery in the real application.

Due to the excellent malleability, high transparency, and outstanding environmental friendliness of sodium alginate, we have chosen it as the natural polymeric carrier (Balakrishnan & Chinthala, 2022). Sodium alginate is a water-soluble polysaccharide with an unbranched linear structure, composed of repeating units of α-L-guluronic acid and β-D-mannuronic acid (Daemi & Barikani, 2012). Under mild conditions, sodium alginate can form three-dimensional hydrogel composites via rapid cross-linking with calcium ions (Zhang & Zhao, 2020) that not only effectively immobilize catalysts but also boost catalytic activities of the composite due to the inherent characteristics of the alginate. Recent advances in the fabrication of sodium alginate-encapsulated g-C3N4 hydrogel beads have predominantly focused on the modification of carbon nitride with inorganic ions, minerals, and metal oxides, including examples such as phosphate, kaolin, and copper oxide (Balakrishnan et al., 2024; Valiyathur et al., 2024). Zhan et al. (2024), integrated N vacancy and O doped g-C3N4 into sodium alginate and achieved remarkable ciprofloxacin removal without catalyst decay after 20 cycles. Nonetheless, among a wide variety of dopants, carbon quantum dots have emerged as one of the most promising candidates for modifying g-C3N4. This is attributed to their exceptional electron acceptor/donor properties and the presence of abundant hydrophilic groups (Wen et al., 2023). Due to the abundance of hydrophilic sites on carbon quantum dots, hydrogen bonding is likely to serve as a critical mediator in the integration of g-C3N4 with alginate molecules, which would effectively form a more stabilized 3D-crosss linking structure without destroying the intrinsic architecture of the components. However, the existing body of literature pertaining to the immobilization of carbon quantum dots/graphitic carbon nitride (CQDs/g-C3N4) onto alginate hydrogel beads is rather limited. Furthermore, the interactive effects of CQDs/g-C3N4 and alginate and their contribute to the enhancement of peroxymonosulfate have not been extensively explored.

In this study, sodium alginate was used to support and immobilize Fe, N-CQDs modified g-C3N4 to develop a photocatalyst with enhanced efficiency, stability, and recoverability. The effects of incorporating iron and nitrogen-doped CQDs/g-C3N4 (FNCCN) into the sodium alginate matrix on the composite's morphology, structure, and photoelectrochemical behavior were investigated. By using gel beads as catalysts and sulfamethoxazole (SMX) as model pollutant, we evaluated the photocatalytic activity and explored the roles of each ingredient. We also examined bead stability, reusability, and the mechanisms involved. LC-MS analysis helped identify degradation intermediates, and the SMX degradation pathways were deduced using Fukui function calculations.

Sulfamethoxazole (SMX, 98.0 %), diethylenetriamine (DETA, 99 %), ferric trichloride hexahydrate (FeCl3 .6H2O, 99 %), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium thiosulfate (Na2SO3), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), sodium alginate (SA, (C6H7NaO6)n (Fig. S1), viscosity (1 %, Brookfield LV, 20 °C) = 200 ± 20 mPa·s, the M/G ratio ≈ 1:1, Mw = 1,273,516 g/mol), calcium chloride (CaCl2) and L-histidine were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Humic acid (HA, 98 %) was provided from J&K Chemical Ltd. (China). Citric acid anhydrous (CA, 99 %), urea (CH4N2O, 99.0 %), Potassium peroxomonosulfate (KHSO5·0.5 KHSO4·0.5 K2SO4, PMS), sodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA-2Na), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), methanol (MeOH), isopropanol (IPA), potassium dichromate (Kr2Cr2O7), sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3), phenol and 2,2,6,6-tetramethy l-4-piperidinol (TMP) and 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). All the above chemicals are analytical grade without further purification.
The preparation of Fe, N-CQDs is delineated in Supporting Information Text S1.

CN and FNCCN were synthesized through a simple thermal polymerization method (Li, Chen, et al., 2022; Zhang, Cao, et al., 2024a). The specific steps are as follows: First, 3 g of urea and a certain amount of 5 mg Fe, N-CQD were uniformly ground in a mortar and then transferred to a crucible. Subsequently, the crucible was heated in a muffle furnace at a heating rate of 5 °C per minute up to 550 °C, maintained at this temperature for 3 h, and then allowed to cool down naturally. The synthesized sample was labeled as FNCCN. Using the same method and steps, CN was obtained without adding any CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the preparation of alginate-stabilized FNCCN composite microbeads (CA/FNCCN) was conducted using the sol-gel method and titration process. With the specific steps as follows: initially, 200 mg of sodium alginate was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, referred to as Solution A, and stirred in a water bath at 60 °C for 1 h until the sodium alginate was uniformly dissolved without any bubble formation. Subsequently, an amount of FNCCN was added to the solution A and stirred for another 1 h to obtain the SA/FNCCN suspension. 2.5 g of CaCl2 was dissolved in 50 mL of water to form Solution B. The SA/FNCCN suspension was then slowly added dropwise to Solution B using a syringe (volume of 5 mL and an outlet diameter of 2 mm), where they rapidly crosslinked to form microbeads. After waiting for 12 h to ensure complete crosslinking, the microbeads were collected, washed with water, and dried overnight at 60 °C to obtain the composite microbeads CA/FNCCN. Based on preliminary experimental results, FNCCN to sodium alginate mass ratios of 0.5:1, 1:1, and 2:1 (w/w) were designed and the materials were named CA/FNCCN-0.5, CA/FNCCN-1, and CA/FNCCN-2 respectively. For comparison, bare alginate beads (CA) and carbon nitride beads without Fe, N-CQDs (CA/CN) were also prepared.
An exhaustive elucidation of the characteristics is delineated in the Supporting Information Text S2.

Sulfamethoxazole (SMX) was chosen as the target pollutant to assess the activation performance of the catalyst. The reactions were conducted in a photochemical reactor (JH-GHX -III, Jihui Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) equipped with a xenon lamp and UV cutoff filters, under ambient temperature conditions maintained by a circulating water system. The initial pH of the SMX solution was adjusted using KOH (0.1 M) and H2SO4 (0.1 M). Typically, 16 mg of the catalyst was added to a test tube containing 20 mL of a 5 ppm SMX solution. The mixture was agitated in the dark for 30 min to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After this period, the light source was activated to initiate the photocatalytic reaction, and PMS (0.5 g/L) was introduced into the tube. Samples were periodically taken from the reaction mixture; each 1 mL sample was immediately mixed with 0.1 mL of Na2S2O3 (0.5 M) to quench the ongoing photocatalytic reaction and then filtered using 0.22 μm water system membrane filters to remove any residual powder. Throughout the degradation process, different gradients of reaction parameters were employed to evaluate their influence on the degradation efficiency of SMX. The samples were analyzed by High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) at a wavelength of 264 nm. The degradation kinetics of SMX were modeled using a pseudo-first-order kinetic equation (Eq. (1)).

where C0 and Ct (mg/L) are the SMX concentrations at the beginning of the photocatalytic experiment and t (min), k1 (min−1) is the rate constant. All experiments were performed three times in parallel and the average values were recorded.

In the ROS scavenging experiment, methanol (MeOH) was used to quench hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and sulfate radicals (SO₄·−). Meanwhile, TBA, p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), EDTA-2Na, and L-histidine acted as scavengers for ·OH, superoxide radicals (·O₂−), holes (h+), and singlet oxygen (1O₂), respectively.
Comprehensive information regarding the Computational methods employed can be found in Supporting Information Text S3.

Upon dropping the uniformly mixed sample—the sodium alginate solution—into the CaCl2 solution, composite beads (wet) were rapidly formed. The resulting wet bead samples were uniformly spherical. The CA beads were clear and transparent, indicating minimal obstruction of light penetration and negligible impact on the photocatalytic efficiency of the encapsulated materials. CA/CN and CA/FNCCN wet beads appeared pale yellow and brownish, respectively, matching the colors of their encapsulated powders, indirectly confirming successful encapsulation (Fig.S2). After dehydration, all beads retained their spherical shape but with rougher surfaces (Fig. 1). The water content of CA was 96.18 %, decreasing in composite microbeads after encapsulation (Table S1). The size and water content of the beads varied with the encapsulated material and preparation ratios. While syringe size and slurry flow rate largely determined the size of wet beads, increasing FNCCN content significantly reduced water content and increased the size of dry beads, suggesting that mixing ratio significantly influences bead diameter.

In an aqueous matrix, the microbead samples sink under gravity and can fully contact contaminants in the water matrix with simple stirring. After the reaction, the microbead samples can be efficiently recovered by simple filtration through a sieve and washing.

To explore the internal cross-linking state between alginate and FNCCN, CN photocatalysts, Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was used to investigate the surface and cross-sectional morphology of CN, FNCCN, CA, CA/CN, and CA/FNCCN. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the surface of CA presented irregular smooth and dense folds. FNCCN and CN exhibited a two-dimensional sheet structure (Fig. 2(a) and Fig. S3). Compared to pure CA, the surfaces of CA/CN and CA/FNCCN beads became rougher, and raised particles caused by the addition of powder catalysts were vaguely visible on the surface (Fig. 2(c ∼ d)). Compared to CA/CN, the surface of CA/FNCCN was looser and had more complex folds, with a higher surface roughness, which can be attributed to the strong structural distortion caused by Fe, N-CQDs on CN. As shown in Fig. 2(e ∼ f), the cross-sectional morphology of CA and CA/FNCCN revealed a porous structure with varying sizes. Compared to CA, CA/FNCCN exhibited a smaller and denser pore network, likely due to the disruption of the original alginate hydrogel network after the introduction of FNCCN. The introduction of FNCCN altered the internal structure and surface roughness of CA, providing a larger contact area for the adsorption of organic pollutants, facilitating the adsorption and diffusion of pollutants, and effectively enhancing the efficiency of pollutant removal. Further analysis of the cross-section of CA/FNCCN through EDS, as shown in Fig. 2(g), revealed that all elements are uniformly dispersed. Beyond the primary elements of carbon nitride, C, N, and O, the presence of Ca and Fe indicates that the FNCCN composite material has been successfully and evenly embedded within the alginate.

FTIR was employed to perform a qualitative analysis of the functional groups present on the surfaces of the samples. As depicted in Fig. 3(a), CN and FNCCN exhibited sharp peaks at 808 cm−1 and broad peaks between 1200–1670 cm−1 and 3000–3700 cm−1, corresponding to the bending vibrations of tri-s-triazine units, stretching vibrations of C-N/C=N, and stretching vibrations of -OH, -NH2, and -NH groups, respectively (Samanta et al., 2019). Similar curves indicated that the modification with Fe, N-CQDs does not significantly change the basic framework of g-C3N4. The broad peak observed in the FTIR spectrum of CA between 3000–3600 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (-OH) groups in the alginate molecular chains (Wang, Zhang, & Li, 2023), In CA/FNCCN, the enhanced stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (-OH) groups are mainly attributed to the interactions between alginate and the doped Fe, N-CQDs in the carbon nitride. This interaction may lead to a greater number of hydroxyl groups participating in hydrogen bond formation, thereby enhancing their signal intensity in the FTIR spectrum (Jing et al., 2022). The characteristic peaks observed at 1604 cm−1 and 1415 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of carboxyl functional groups in alginate, respectively (Gao et al., 2020; Tamer et al., 2022). The sharp peaks at 1074 cm−1 and 1021 cm−1 are primarily due to the stretching vibrations of CO and C-O-C bonds (Wang et al., 2024). CA/FNCCN incorporates most of the functional groups from both CA and FNCCN, with the appearance of corresponding characteristic peaks reaffirming that the FNCCN composite material has been successfully and uniformly immobilized within the alginate. The slight red shift at 1604 cm−1 in the carboxyl group might be due to the interaction between the -COO groups in the alginate and FNCCN through hydrogen bonding. Furthermore, carboxyl groups may form complexes with metals or other molecules, leading to changes in electron density and thus shifting the position of the absorption peaks, which reflects the formation of Fe-alginate complexes (da Silva Fernandes et al., 2018; Dash et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2023). Compared to FNCCN, the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in CA/FNCCN were likely to significantly promote the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, which will be further explored in sections 3.3 and 3.4.

The XRD patterns of CA, CA/CN, CA/FNCCN, CN and FNCCN were shown in Fig. 3(b). FNCCN and CN exhibited two distinct diffraction peaks at 13.1° and 27.5°, corresponding to the (100) plane of the s-triazine units and the interlayer stacking of the (002) plane used for conjugated aromatic systems (Mao et al., 2022). Compared to CN, the intensity of two diffraction peaks in FNCCN decreased, indicating that the incorporation of Fe, N-CQDs disrupted the periodic arrangement of the basic units in the carbon nitride, but did not change its crystal structure (Zhang, Cao, et al., 2024b). CA was semi-crystalline with weak crystallinity, and its weaker diffraction peaks at 14.2°, 20.7°, and 40.3° correspond to the reflections of the (200) plane of polygalacturonic acid, the (100) plane of glucuronic acid units, and amorphous halos, respectively (Askarieh et al., 2022; Ben Ayed et al., 2023; Hassan et al., 2023). Apart from the reduction in peak intensity due to the encapsulation by CA, the positions and shapes of the diffraction peaks corresponding to CN and FNCCN in the XRD patterns of CA/CN and CA/FNCCN did not show significant changes, indicating that CA, as an excellent carrier material, did not affect the expression of crystal facets of the encapsulated active materials during the dispersion process. By calculating the crystallinity of different materials (Fig. S4), it can be observed that the hydrogen bonding formed between sodium alginate and Fe, N-CQDs after encapsulation with CA enhances the crystallinity of CA/FNCCN, thereby improving its photocatalytic performance (Li et al., 2024).

Upon thermal exposure, alginates are prone to decomposition. To explore the influence of powder catalysts on the thermal stability of alginates, TGA was conducted on CA, CA/CN, and CA/FNCCN. As illustrated in Fig. 3(c), the mass of CA, CA/CN, and CA/FNCCN decreased progressively with an increase in temperature. The TGA curves of the samples can be primarily divided into three stages, with each stage's mass change and the percentage of residue change detailed in Table 1 . In the first stage, CA exhibits a mass loss of approximately 24.1 %, mainly occurring between 30.5 and 200 °C, which is attributed to the vaporization of water molecules within the CA beads (Nasrullah et al., 2018). The second stage sees a mass loss of about 25.15 %, attributable to the simultaneous de-polymerization and bond cleavage (glucoside bond breaking, dihydroxylation and decarboxylation) between 200 and 320 °C (Germanos et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2016). The third stage, occurring between 320 and 680 °C, involves a mass loss of about 11.90 %, related to the oxidation and pyrolysis of the polymer and intermediate products formed during the second stage (Belhouchat et al., 2017). Compared to CA/CN, CA/FNCCN exhibits slightly inferior thermal stability at temperatures below 550 °C, a phenomenon possibly due to structural distortions in g-C3N4 induced by the introduction of Fe, N-CQDs. Despite CA/FNCCN and CA/CN experiencing higher mass losses at the final temperature of 800 °C compared to CA alone, they show a significantly reduced rate of weight loss across the majority of the temperature range (30.5 to 700 °C), especially below 550 °C. These results indicated that loading CN and FNCCN onto alginates, leveraging the inherent thermal stability of carbon nitride, can significantly mitigate the issue of alginate's inherent poor thermal stability.

The surface chemical states of CA, CA/CN, CA/FNCCN, and FNCCN were investigated using XPS. The XPS full spectra of the 4 materials were shown in Fig. 4(a), indicating the presence of elements such as C, N, and O in all samples. It is noteworthy that in FNCCN, the O1s peak is significantly lower than that of other materials due to the trace amounts of Fe, N-CQDs doping. Compared to CA and FNCCN, the detection of Ca and the sharp increase in the contents of O and N elements in the CA/FNCCN sample confirmed the successful combination of alginate and FNCCN. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of Fe, N-CQDs may be the reason for the slightly higher content of O element in CA/FNCCN compared to CA/CN (Taghiloo et al., 2024; Zhang, Shao, et al., 2024). As shown in Fig. 4(b), the high-resolution C1s spectra of the samples, revealed that the fitting peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.64 eV, and 288.40 eV for CA correspond to the CC, CO, and CO bonds, respectively (Liu et al., 2023). The C1s spectra of CA/CN, CA/FNCCN, and FNCCN all had fitting peaks belonging to C-C/C=C, CO, and N-C=H (Cao et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2024). Among them, the relative contents of C-C/C=C and CO were lower in FNCCN. In contrast, the relative contents of these two functional groups significantly increased in CA/FNCCN, further verifying the structure analysis of the full spectrum and proving the successful combination between alginate and FNCCN (Zhan et al., 2024).

Since CA theoretically does not contain N, only the N1s spectra of CA/CN, CA/FNCCN, and FNCCN were analyzed. In previous studies, we validated through DFT calculations and XPS analysis that Fe, N-CQDs in FNCCN were integrated into g-C3N4 via FeN chemical bonds (the structure from DFT formation energy calculations was shown in Fig. S5 and Fig. S6) (Zhang, Shao, et al., 2024). The formation of FeN bonds had facilitated a tighter connection between the Fe, N-CQDs and g-C3N4 components, reducing the charge transfer barrier. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the N1s high-resolution spectrum of CA/FNCCN can be fitted to four peaks at 398.62, 399.30, 401.03, and 404.20 eV, corresponding to pyridinic nitrogen (C-N=C), triazine nitrogen (NC3)/N-Fe, tertiary nitrogen (C-N-H), and oxidized nitrogen. The presence of NFe indicated that alginate did not change the coordination structure of the FeN bond in FNCCN (Hu et al., 2019). The introduction of Fe, N-CQDs can change the electron cloud density around N, possibly causing a significant chemical shift in the binding energy of the N1s orbital peaks in CA/FNCCN compared to CA/CN (Zhang, Shao, et al., 2024). The high-resolution Fe2p spectra of FNCCN and CA/FNCCN (Fig. 4(d)), indicated the presence of both Fe(II) and Fe(III) in CA/FNCCN, suggesting that the solidification of alginate does not change the valence state of Fe in FNCCN. The interfacial interaction between alginate and FNCCN may lead to a decrease in the electron cloud density of CA/FNCCN.

Previous studies have shown that the introduction of Fe atoms leads to a shift in the O1s binding energy to a higher region, indicating a reduction in the electron density of neighboring N and O atoms. However, the structural characteristics of the highly hydrophilic functional groups in the carbon dots remain unchanged (Zhang, Shao, et al., 2024). Nevertheless, the high hydrophilic functional group structure characteristic of carbon dots remains unaltered. The change of oxygen-containing functional groups can determine the formation of hydrogen bonding to some extent (Kerber et al., 1996), as shown in Fig. 4(e), the addition of FNCCN to CA resulted in an increment in the amount of oxygen-containing functional groups (Fig. 4a) and a decrease in the binding energy by 0.13 eV, which suggests that hydrogen bonding is formed internally by alginate-encapsulated FNCCN, and the DFT simulation (binding of sodium alginate (fragments) to the carbon dots in the nitrided carbon) also provides the visual image of the H-bonding formation (Fig. S10). The high interfacial binding strength between CA and FNCCN allowed FNCCN to be effectively fixed on the surface of CA and well maintained the coordination structure and chemical valence of Fe, creating favorable conditions for the subsequent photocatalytic reaction process in the activation of PMS and efficient recycling of the catalyst (Kou et al., 2024).

The light absorption capabilities of different photocatalysts were assessed using UV–vis-DRS technology. In Fig. 5(a), although CA can absorb ultraviolet light in the 250–350 nm range, its ability to absorb visible light is relatively weak. However, when it was encapsulated with photocatalyst such as CN and FNCCN, its light absorption in both visible and ultraviolet regions was significantly enhanced. The up-conversion photoluminescence effect of Fe, N-CQDs was the main reason for the noticeable redshift of the absorption edge of CA/FNCCN compared to CA/CN (Chen et al., 2021). Compared to FNCCN, the absorption intensity of CA/FNCCN in the visible region was greatly increased, a boost likely due to the introduction of alginate enhancing the internal light reflection and diffusion within the composite material, effectively promoting the capture and utilization of light energy (Hao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023).

Based on eq. (S1), The band gap values for CA/CN, CA/FNCCN, and FNCCN were 2.41 eV, 1.80 eV, and 2.18 eV, respectively (Fig. 5(b)). Typically, photocatalyst materials with smaller band gaps demonstrate higher photocatalytic activity as they can absorb and utilize more visible light, thereby generating more photo-generated carriers (Qi et al., 2022). This result indicated that the synergistic effect of alginate with Fe, N-CQDs can rationally improve the band gap of the composite material, making it more photocatalytically active.

Alginate encapsulation's effect on the band structure of the composite material was assessed by determining the valence band positions of CA/CN, CA/FNCCN, and FNCCN using VB-XPS. This analysis reveals how the surface electronic configuration influences photocatalytic performance by examining the interaction between the material's surface and light, as shown in Fig. 5(c). The valence band positions for CA/CN, CA/FNCCN, and FNCCN were 2.07 eV, 1.43 eV, and 1.82 eV, respectively. Further, based on the band gap and valence band results, the conduction band positions of the photocatalysts were identified (eq. (S2)), and a schematic diagram of the photocatalyst band structure was drawn (Fig. S7). It aided in the subsequent analysis of the mechanism of free radical generation.

Transient photocurrent response, EIS, and PL spectroscopy were also employed to investigate the interfacial electron transport performance and the separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers of the photocatalysts. Fig. 5(e) presented the photocurrent tests for FNCCN, CA/FNCCN, and CA/CN, with the photocurrent intensity in the order of CA/CN < FNCCN < CA/FNCCN. Fe, N-CQDs possessed good electron reservoir properties, capable of suppressing the recombination of photo-generated carriers in the catalyst, which was the reason for CA/FNCCN has a higher photocurrent intensity than CA/CN (Si et al., 2021). Comparing FNCCN with CA/FNCCN, it was found that alginate also had the potential to reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs in the photocatalyst. This was because alginate, as a natural polysaccharide with a three-dimensional network structure and electron-capturing hydroxyl groups, can effectively improve the interfacial characteristics of the composite photocatalyst, promote carrier transport, suppress photo-generated carrier recombination, and thereby enhance the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction (Hao et al., 2021; Mandal et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2021).

The EIS spectra of FNCCN, CA/FNCCN, and CA/CN were shown in Fig. 5 (d). CA/FNCCN exhibited the smallest arc radius, while CA/CN had the largest. Photocatalysts with a smaller arc radius typically exhibit faster interfacial charge transfer rates (Jiang et al., 2021). This result confirmed that the introduction of Fe, N-CQDs and alginate can reduce charge loss between catalyst components, favoring an increase in charge transfer rate.

The PL spectra of FNCCN, CA/FNCCN, and CA/CN were shown in Fig. 5(f). CA/CN had the highest peak intensity, indicating a more severe recombination of photo-generated carriers. Compared to CA/CN, the peak intensity of CA/FNCCN significantly decreased, indicating that Fe, N-CQDs, acting as electron acceptors, can effectively suppress carrier recombination (Jin et al., 2021). The alginate-encapsulated FNCCN, compared to FNCCN alone, showed even lower peak intensity, indicating that alginate can further improve charge transfer, effectively suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs in the photocatalyst. These results were consistent with the above analysis of photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance.

Before exploring the degradation effects of different reaction systems on SMX, the preparation ratio of CA/FNCCN was first optimized (Fig. 6(a)). In the CA/FNCCN-0.5 + PMS + vis system, the degradation efficiency for SMX was 90.25 % at 40 min and 94.64 % at 60 min (k = 0.6778 min−1). When the mixing ratio of sodium alginate to FNCCN was increased to 1:1, the CA/FNCCN-1 + PMS + vis system could nearly degrade SMX completely within 40 min (k = 0.0951 min−1). The increase in degradation efficiency was attributed to FNCCN acting as the primary active substance in the reaction; increasing its proportion raises the number of active sites, accelerating the catalytic reaction process. However, when the proportion of FNCCN further increased to 2:1, the reaction rate further increased (k = 0.9761 min−1), but the growth trend significantly slowed. This slowdown was because some catalysts were encapsulated inside the CA/FNCCN beads, affecting their catalytic degradation to maximize effects. Considering degradation efficiency and resource utilization, CA/FNCCN-1 was used for subsequent experiments.

The degradation efficiency and related kinetic constants of different systems are shown in Fig. 6(b) and Fig. 6(c). With SMX as the antibiotic molecule, the adsorption removal effect of FNCCN and CA/FNCCN under dark reaction conditions on SMX was investigated. The adsorption rate of FNCCN powder on SMX was relatively low, at only 15.27 %. After encapsulation with alginate, the adsorption rate of CA/FNCCN on SMX increased to 20.40 %, which confirmed that the oxygen-containing functional groups present on the surface of alginate can promote the adsorption of pollutants on the catalyst surface (Balakrishnan et al., 2024; Du et al., 2022).

Upon introducing illumination, CA degradation effect on SMX was approximately 12.17 % (k = 0.0004 min−1) at 60 min, which did not show a significant improvement compared to the removal efficiency at the end of the dark reaction (10.71 %). This indicated that CA struggles to generate reactive species under illumination conditions. When CN was encapsulated, the degradation effect on SMX improved to 19.13 % at 60 min (k = 0.0013 min−1), due to the photocatalytic activity of CN itself leading to the degradation of SMX. Compared to CA/CN, when encapsulating FNCCN, the degradation effect on SMX at 60 min improved to 27.67 % (k = 0.0026 min−1), confirming the modification effect of Fe, N-CQDs on the CN photocatalyst. At the same time, the efficiency of FNCCN in photocatalytically degrading SMX within the same timeframe was 22.93 %, slightly lower than the photocatalytic effect of CA/FNCCN. This suggested that CA had the potential to alter the band gap of the photocatalyst and reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs, consistent with the results of the aforementioned photoelectrical performance tests.

Using PMS as an oxidant and incorporating it into the photocatalytic system, the effect of different photocatalysts on activating PMS to degrade SMX was explored (Fig. 6(c)). It was challenging for SMX to degrade merely through PMS oxidation under light conditions. Upon introducing a catalyst into the system, the efficiency of CA in degrading SMX through photocatalytic activation of PMS over 60 min reached about 20.69 % (k = 0.0017 min−1), which marked an improvement compared to its direct photocatalytic degradation efficiency of SMX (k = 0.0004 min−1). However, as a natural polysaccharide, CA did not directly participate in the activation of PMS (Cong et al., 2022). When encapsulating photocatalysts such as CN and FNCCN, the efficiency of the catalyst in photocatalically activating PMS to degrade SMX significantly increased. The degradation efficiencies of CA/CN and CA/FNCCN for SMX were 81.65 % (k = 0.0257 min−1) and 99.52 % (k = 0.0951 min−1), respectively. The synergy index of CA/FNCCN was calculated according to Eq. (S4), and the result was 3.003 > 1, which indicated that there was a synergistic interaction between photocatalysis and heterogeneous Fenton reactions in the system (Liu et al., 2022). The synergistic interaction present between the systems is the main reason for the substantial increase in SMX degradation efficiency.

However, compared to FNCCN + PMS + vis system, the degradation efficiency of SMX by CA/FNCCN + PMS + vis system was slightly reduced. This was mainly because, although alginate, as a solidifying carrier, can significantly enhance the photoelectrochemical activity of FNCCN, the key species Fe ratio in CA/FNCCN decreases compared to the same mass of FNCCN, which inevitably affected the catalytic activity. To verify the enhancing effect of alginate in the system, experiments were conducted again using the same mass of FNCCN encapsulated in CA/FNCCN as a benchmark (marked as FNCCN(d)). The results showed that the degradation rate of SMX by CA/FNCCN activated PMS was much higher than that of the FNCCN powder encapsulated within it. Overall, alginate, as an excellent carrier material, not only promoted more efficient photocatalytic reactions of FNCCN but also facilitated the rapid activation of PMS.

To determine the optimal conditions for SMX degradation in the CA/FNCCN + PMS + Vis system, the impact of various reaction parameters on the efficiency of SMX degradation was investigated. The details are delineated in Supporting Information Text S5.

Unlike CN and FNCCN powders, CA/FNCCN did not require centrifugation or other complex recovery treatments; it was efficiently recovered through simple filtration and washing. The reusability of CA/FNCCN was further evaluated through cycling experiments, with the results presented in Fig. 7(a). CA/FNCCN maintained high catalytic activity throughout the cycles, with SMX degradation efficiencies of 99.18 %, 96.23 %, 94.15 %, 91.26 %, 89.61 %, 89.56 %, 89.54 %, 89.5 %, 89.47 % and 89.39 % over ten cycles, respectively. These results demonstrate that CA/FNCCN exhibits good reusability and has potential for long-term application.

Stability was another important criterion for evaluating the practical application of catalysts. For metal-based or metal-containing catalysts, the inevitable leaching of metals was a significant factor contributing to their poor stability. Using ICP-MS technology, the leaching concentrations of iron ions in the FNCCN + PMS + vis and CA/FNCCN + PMS + vis systems during the reaction process were assessed. The results, as shown in Fig. 7(b), indicated that the iron ion leaching concentrations of FNCCN and CA/FNCCN gradually stabilized after 20 min of reaction and reached their maximum values at 40 min. The maximum leaching concentrations were 341.69 μg/L for FNCCN and 139.24 μg/L for CA/FNCCN. To eliminate the possibility that the higher iron ion leaching concentration of FNCCN (d) was due to differences in iron content in the same mass of FNCCN and CA/FNCCN, the iron ion leaching concentration of an equivalent amount of FNCCN encapsulated in CA/FNCCN was additionally measured, with its maximum value being 242.32 μg/L. This value was still significantly higher than the maximum iron ion leaching concentration of CA/FNCCN, indicating that the encapsulation by alginate not only facilitated the recovery of the catalyst but also enhanced the stability of the powdered photocatalyst, effectively suppressing the leaching of metal ions from the catalyst. The metal ions leached into the aqueous solution could be reabsorbed into the catalyst material by forming stable chemical bonds with the abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in the molecular structure of alginate, thus ensuring safety in use and effectively reducing environmental pollution. The XRD spectra and crystallinity of CA/FNCCN before and after the reaction, as shown in Fig. 7(c) and Fig. S11, exhibit similar characteristic peaks and peak intensities, with only minimal differences in crystallinity. This indicates that CA/FNCCN can maintain its structural integrity during the reaction process, further validating its superior stability.

EPR technology was utilized to preliminarily identify the main reactive oxygen species in different systems. As depicted in Fig. 8(a), under visible light conditions, PMS was hardly directly activated to produce ·OH and SO4·−. Therefore, the characteristic peak signals of ·OH and SO4·− were not detected when using DMPO as a spin trap. After the addition of CA/FNCCN, the reaction of CA/FNCCN with PMS produced a strong oxidizing substance that directly oxidized DMPO, and a sevenfold peak of DMPO-X appeared, indicating the generation of reactive oxygen species in the system (He et al., 2021). Meanwhile, the appearance of the DMPO-·O2 − signal in Fig. 8(b) confirmed the generation of ·O2 − in the CA/FNCCN + PMS + vis system. Moreover, the emergence of a typical triplet peak in Fig. 8(c) verified the production of 1O2 during this process.

To investigate the main mechanism behind the degradation of SMX in the CA/FNCCN + PMS + vis system, further experiments were conducted using quenching agents to identify the reactive species within the system, with the results shown in Fig. 8(d). Compared to other scavengers, L-histidine exhibited the most significant inhibition of the SMX degradation effect, reducing the removal rate of SMX from an initial 99.52 % to 43.89 %, indicating that 1O2 was the primary reactive species in the system. EDTA-2Na, serving as a scavenger for h+, showed the second most significant inhibition effect on SMX degradation after L-histidine, suggesting that h+ was a secondary reactive species in the reaction. IPA, used as a quencher for ·OH, reduced the removal rate of SMX to 88.54 %, indicating the presence of ·OH in the system. However, in Fig. 5(g), it was found that the VB value of CA/FNCCN was 1.43 eV (< 1.99 eV), making it difficult for ·OH to be generated through the oxidation of H2O by holes, suggesting the existence of other conversion reactions, possibly involving ·O2 − and SO4·−. Phenol, serving as a quencher for both ·OH and SO4·−, reduced the removal rate of SMX to 74.18 % upon addition, lower than the degradation efficiency when IPA was added, indicating the presence of SO4·− in the system. After adding p-BQ, the degradation efficiency of SMX dropped to 70.35 %, indicating the presence of ·O2 − in the system. To further determine the source of ·O2 −, experiments were reconducted with N2 purging in the reaction system. Compared to the use of p-BQ alone, the removal efficiency further decreased, suggesting that ·O2 − was not solely the product of photogenerated electrons reducing dissolved oxygen, and there were other pathways for ·O2 − generation, possibly from the conversion of ·OH. In summary, in the CA/FNCCN + PMS + vis system, h+, ·O2 −, 1O2, ·OH, and SO4·− all participated in the degradation of SMX, with their contributions to SMX removal being in the order of 1O2 > h+ > ·O2 − > SO4·− > ·OH.

XPS analysis was performed on the samples after the reactions of CA/FNCCN, CA/FNCCN + vis, and CA/FNCCN + vis + PMS had finished. The ratios of Fe3+/Fe2+ relative contents were calculated based on the obtained Fe2p XPS spectra. As illustrated in Fig. S9, the ratios of Fe(III)/Fe(II) for CA/FNCCN (fresh and after use), CA/FNCCN + vis, and CA/FNCCN + vis + PMS were 80.12 %, 78.98 %, 71.45 %, and 84.57 %, respectively. These findings suggested that PMS, acting as an electron acceptor, could be directly activated by the Fe(III)/Fe(II) in CA/FNCCN. The reductive electrons generated under light exposure facilitated the valence state transition from Fe(III) to Fe(II) within CA/FNCCN, thus accelerating the activation rate of PMS.

Based on the analysis above, we proposed a possible mechanism for pollutant degradation in the CA/FNCCN + PMS + vis system, as illustrated in Fig. 9 . Fe, N-CQDs were embedded into the carbon framework of g-C3N4 via FeN bonds to form FNCCN. Sodium alginate, serving as a solidifying carrier, was connected to FNCCN through hydrogen bond interactions between its surface carboxyl group and the NH groups in FNCCN, resulting in the formation of CA/FNCCN (Balakrishnan et al., 2024). The introduction of sodium alginate effectively preserved the coordination structure and chemical valence state of iron within FNCCN and enriched the CA/FNCCN surface with a high concentration of oxygen-containing functional groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl groups). This provided favorable conditions for PMS activation and efficient recovery of the catalyst. Under dark conditions, CA/FNCCN can activate PMS to produce reactive oxygen species directly through the coexistence of Fe(III) and Fe(II) within the material (Eq. (14) to (16)). Additionally, the sodium alginate in CA/FNCCN can mediate direct electron transfer between SMX and PMS (Cong et al., 2022). The synergistic effect of these processes ultimately enabled the efficient removal of SMX by CA/FNCCN in the absence of light. Benefited from the up-conversion photoluminescence of Fe, N-CQDs, and the capability of hydroxyl groups on the surface of sodium alginate to capture photogenerated electrons, CA/FNCCN exhibited a broad visible light absorption range and a lower rate of photogenerated carrier recombination. Under the illumination of visible light, CA/FNCCN generated both photogenerated electrons and holes ((Eq. (17)). Some of the photogenerated electrons were transferred to Fe, N-CQDs via the FeN bond, accelerating the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle and thereby speeding up the efficient activation of PMS ((Eq. (18) to (19)). Meanwhile, some electrons that were not transferred quickly were stored within the three-dimensional polysaccharide network of sodium alginate, waiting for further reactions. The synergistic action of sodium alginate and Fe, N-CQDs maximized the utilization efficiency of photogenerated carriers in g-C3N4. Additionally, ·OH and ·O2 − can be generated (Eq. (20) to (24)). 1O2, as the main reactive oxygen species in the system, can be produced by the reaction of O2/·O2 − with h+ or ·O2 − with SO4·− (Eq. (25) to (26)). SMX was oxidatively degraded into small molecular compounds, CO2, and H2O under the influence of direct electron transfer and the reactive oxygen species 1O2, SO4·−, ·O2 −, and ·OH (Eq. (27)).

To highlight the advantages of the CA/FNCCN catalyst, we conducted a comparative study, as shown in Table 2 . In most photocatalytic systems, the degradation of pollutants typically suffers from longer degradation times and relatively reduced efficiency. In contrast, the CA/FNCCN catalyst demonstrates significant superiority in the remediation of organic pollutants, effectively minimizing both time and energy consumption, making it an excellent photocatalyst.

Based on the optimization of SMX molecular structure (Fig. 10a), the electrostatic potential (Fig. 10b), HOMO and LUMO orbitals (Fig. 10c) and Fukui index (Fig. 10d) of SMX molecules were calculated to determine the reaction-active site of SMX. The electrostatic potential of the SMX molecule showed the electron-rich regions with electrophilic activity in blue, and the lack of electronic and nucleophilic activity in red. Once atoms combined to create molecules, they gave rise to molecular orbitals that can be represented by the LUMO and HOMO. In General, the functional group of HOMO were prone to electrophilic reactions, while the LUMO were prone to nucleophilic reactions (Yang et al., 2022). Combining Fig. 10b with Fig. 10c, the N4 and S8 regions are prominently displayed as electron-rich areas, while the O15 region is clearly an electron-deficient zone. The functional groups in the SMX molecule, such as O3-N4 = C5 and O16 = S8 = O17, significantly influence the distribution of electron density. A smaller HOMO-LUMO gap often indicates that the molecule is more reactive and more likely to undergo electron transfer reactions, leading to its degradation. Nonetheless, HOMO and LUMO can only provide a rough indication of the regions involved and may not precisely determine which atoms will undergo nucleophilic, electrophilic, or radical reactions (Li, Lu, et al., 2022). To further elucidate the reactive active site of SMX, Fukui-index were calculated in Table. S4, including f−, f0 and f+, which stand for electrophilic attack index, radical attack index and nucleophilic attack index, respectively. From Table. S5, can be clearly seen that C11, C13 and C9 had a strong electrophilicity. S9, C14, C10 and C12 had a strong nucleophilicity. N15 was more vulnerable to radical and electrophilic attacks.

According to the results of DFT calculation and LC-MS detection, the possible degradation pathway of SMX in CA/FNCCN +vis +PMS system was proposed. As shown in Fig. 10e, the decomposition of SMX was mainly carried out by hydrolysis, self-coupling, hydroxylation and oxidation. In pathway I, the SN bond of SMX was vulnerable to nucleophilic attack, which caused S8-N7 to break to generate P1(m/z = 99). Conjugation and hydroxylation can occur simultaneously on the P1. On the one hand, the -NH2 group produced P2 through the coupling reaction, while the reactive oxygen species were able to further oxidize P2 (m/z = 193) to form P3 (m/z = 209). On the other hand, the surface hydroxylation of P1 generated P4 (m/z = 115) and P5 (m/z = 133). In pathway II, N15 was the most active site with f− value of 0.127 in SMX. It was easy to be attacked by h+ and 1O2 to form P6 (m/z = 284) and further oxidized and degraded to P7 (m/z = 99). In pathway III, SMX formed hydroxylation product P8 (m/z = 270) at C6, which broke the S8-N7 bond to generate P9 (m/z = 156) under the continuous attack of reactive oxygen species. In pathway IV, SMX was substituted by hydroxyl group to form P10 (m/z = 284) during the electrophilic reaction, and the hydroxyl group was broken to generate P11(m/z = 239). Finally, all intermediates can be further mineralized into small molecules, CO2 and H2O.

In this study, a hydrogel bead (CA/FNCCN) was constructed, which exhibited both high catalytic activity and good recoverability. Within this structure, Fe, N-CQD was embedded into the g-C3N4 framework through FeN chemical bonds to form FNCCN. Sodium alginate, served as the solidifying carrier, bonding to FNCCN through hydrogen bond between the carboxyl groups on its surface and the hydrophilic sites within FNCCN. This bonding process formed CA/FNCCN, which not only immobilized FNCCN but also effectively preserved the iron's coordination structure and chemical valence within FNCCN, and enriched the surface of CA/FNCCN with high concentrations of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Benefiting from the optical properties of Fe, N-CQDs, the three-dimensional network structure of alginate, and its surface-rich oxygen-containing functional groups, the optical properties of g-C3N4 were effectively improved, and the utilization of photogenerated carriers was maximized. Under illumination, CA/FNCCN exhibited excellent PMS activation and SMX degradation performance, achieving a removal rate of 99.18 % for SMX within 40 min, with a degradation rate constant of 0.0951 min−1. In the system, the main active species were 1O2 and h+, and aside from the degradation of SMX through the attack of these active species, direct electron transfer between PMS and SMX mediated by alginate also contributed to the removal of SMX. Cycling experiments and stability tests showed that the three-dimensional hydrogel beads formed by alginate as a solidifying carrier not only effectively resolved the issue of recovering FNCCN powder but also significantly enhanced the stability of FNCCN, effectively inhibiting the leaching of metal ions. Additionally, the degradation intermediates of SMX were identified through LC-MS analysis, and the degradation pathways of SMX were deduced by combining calculations using the Fukui function. This study provides a theoretical and experimental basis for designing g-C3N4-based photocatalysts for the treatment of persistent pollutants in water bodies. In the future, emphasis will be placed on the application of the photocatalytic system in real water bodies.

Haiyan Yang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiao Cao: Validation, Investigation. Yiyang He: Validation. Xinchao Zhang: Validation, Data curation. Pingping Zhang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xin Wang: Data curation. Yuehong Liu: Investigation. Suyun Xu: Resources, Investigation. Yuyin Fang: Investigation. Lin Gu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
